items.stem: The children ___ playing at the void deck.
items.rationale: 'children' is plural, so use 'are'.

items.stem: My brother ___ to school by MRT.
items.rationale: 'brother' is singular, so the verb takes -s: goes.

items.stem: Kavitha and Siti ___ good friends.
items.rationale: Two people joined by 'and' are plural, so use 'are'.

items.stem: The dog next door ___ loudly every night.
items.rationale: 'dog' is singular, so the verb takes -s: barks.

items.stem: Neither the teacher nor the pupils ___ aware of the change.
items.rationale: In 'neither...nor...', the verb agrees with the nearer noun ('pupils', plural) → were.

items.stem: Each of the boys ___ a water bottle.
items.rationale: 'Each' is singular, so use 'has'.

items.stem: She ___ her homework every evening.
items.rationale: 'She' is singular, so use 'does'.

items.stem: There ___ many people at the wet market.
items.rationale: 'many people' is plural, so use 'are'.

items.stem: The books on the shelf ___ dusty.
items.rationale: The subject is 'books' (plural), not 'shelf', so use 'are'.

items.stem: The baby ___ when it is hungry.
items.rationale: 'baby' is singular, so the verb takes -es: cries.

items.stem: The pupils ___ their books on the desk.
items.rationale: 'pupils' is plural, so the verb has no -s: place.

items.stem: My sister ___ the piano beautifully.
items.rationale: 'sister' is singular, so the verb takes -s: plays.

items.stem: The bag of apples ___ heavy.
items.rationale: The subject is 'bag' (singular), not 'apples', so use 'is'.

items.stem: Ravi and his cousin ___ at the same school.
items.rationale: Two people joined by 'and' are plural, so use 'study'.

items.stem: One of the windows ___ broken.
items.rationale: The subject is 'One', which is singular, so use 'is'.

items.stem: Neither Siti nor her sisters ___ coming today.
items.rationale: In 'neither...nor...', the verb agrees with the nearer noun ('sisters', plural) → are.

items.stem: The netball team ___ practising hard for the match.
items.rationale: 'team' is treated as one group here, so use 'is'.

items.stem: The cats ___ sleeping on the mat.
items.rationale: 'cats' is plural, so use 'are'.

items.stem: Every pupil ___ a locker in the corridor.
items.rationale: 'Every pupil' is singular, so use 'has'.

items.stem: Mathematics ___ my favourite subject.
items.rationale: 'Mathematics' looks plural but is a single subject, so use 'is'.

items.stem: The birds ___ sweetly in the morning.
items.rationale: 'birds' is plural, so the verb has no -s: sing.

items.stem: She and I ___ going to the library.
items.rationale: 'She and I' is plural, so use 'are'.

items.stem: Neither the pupils nor the teacher ___ ready.
items.rationale: The verb agrees with the nearer noun ('teacher', singular) → was.

items.stem: The old man ___ in the park every morning.
items.rationale: 'man' is singular, so the verb takes -s: walks.

GR-sva-simple.iCanStatement: I can make the verb agree with the subject.
GR-sva-simple.explanation: A singular subject takes a verb with -s (the dog barks); a plural subject takes a verb without -s (the dogs bark). Watch out: with 'neither...nor...', the verb agrees with the noun nearer to it.
